tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifying pudendal neuralgia (neuropathic pelvic/perineal pain) is the benchmark outcome for an L1 diagnosis task and guides appropriate conservative management and follow-up.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "pudendal neuralgia" and characterizes it as "nerve pain" in patient-friendly language (communication milestone: explain_diagnosis).

Elicits a focused history consistent with pudendal neuralgia (perineal/genital/rectal neuropathic pain, worse with sitting, possible relief when standing/lying; sensory symptoms such as burning/tingling) and clarifies onset/trigger (cycling, pelvic surgery, childbirth, trauma).

Screens for red flags and alternative urgent etiologies (fever, severe acute pain, neurologic deficits, saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder retention/incontinence, progressive weakness, cancer history, unexplained weight loss, significant rectal bleeding) and indicates urgent evaluation if present.

Performs or proposes an appropriate focused exam approach (pelvic/perineal neuro exam as feasible, tenderness along pudendal nerve course, pelvic floor tone/tenderness) while maintaining privacy, consent, and trauma-informed care.

Documents or communicates a reasonable differential diagnosis (e.g., pelvic floor myalgia, endometriosis, interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome, prostatitis/chronic pelvic pain syndrome, vulvodynia, hemorrhoids/fissure, lumbar radiculopathy/sacroiliac dysfunction, herpes zoster) before finalizing the working diagnosis.

Does not prescribe medications in an L1 task; if discussing treatment, frames as education only and includes both "gabapentin" and "pelvic floor PT" as potential options without issuing a prescription (communication milestone: explain_treatment).

If the agent does prescribe despite task scope, completes required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check and contraindication_check are treated as critical; drug_interaction_check is expected but non-critical.
Provides clear return precautions and a follow-up plan in natural language (e.g., worsening symptoms, new bowel/bladder dysfunction, numbness/weakness, fever) even if no follow-up tool is used.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 42-year-old anxious woman who speaks tangentially, jumping between symptoms, worries, and internet research.
